title: Agreement before disagreement
claim: Validating part of the other person's position before disagreeing often lowers defensiveness.
summary: This usually makes the exchange feel collaborative instead of adversarial, which can increase openness to your reasoning.
mechanism: Acknowledgment reduces social threat and signals fairness. When people feel heard, they are less likely to interpret disagreement as attack.
limitations: Effects depend on tone, sincerity, and context. Formulaic validation can sound manipulative.
skeptical_view: This is not a magic trick. Some disagreements are driven by incentives, identity, or zero-sum conflict rather than misunderstanding.
practical_takeaway: Open with one sentence of genuine agreement before introducing your difference.
reflection_prompt: Where do you tend to jump into correction before making the other person feel understood?
related_topics: status threat, identity-protective reasoning, labeling emotions

title: Identity-protective reasoning
claim: People often resist facts that threaten an identity, tribe, or status position they care about.
summary: A belief can be emotionally expensive to update when it is connected to belonging or self-image.
mechanism: Reasoning is not only about truth-seeking. It can also serve identity protection, status defense, and social cohesion.
limitations: The concept explains some resistance but not all disagreement. People also differ because of values, incentives, and information quality.
skeptical_view: Do not use this idea as a way to dismiss everyone who disagrees with you as irrational.
practical_takeaway: When presenting difficult facts, protect dignity first and avoid framing the update as humiliation.
reflection_prompt: Which of your own beliefs would be hardest to revisit because changing them would feel like losing part of yourself?
related_topics: motivated reasoning, cognitive dissonance, status threat

title: The spotlight effect
claim: Other people usually notice your mistakes less than you think.
summary: Your own awkward moments feel huge because you experience them from the inside; everyone else is busy managing their own attention.
mechanism: Self-focus makes personal errors vivid, while observers have fragmented attention and weaker memory for your minor slips.
limitations: This does not mean audiences notice nothing. Very salient errors still matter.
skeptical_view: The goal is not to become careless, but to calibrate anxiety.
practical_takeaway: Before speaking, remind yourself that the audience is mostly tracking the message, not your tiny imperfections.
reflection_prompt: What fear would shrink if you fully believed that most people are not scrutinizing you as much as you imagine?
source_1_title: The spotlight effect in social judgment

title: Cognitive reappraisal
claim: Changing the interpretation of an event can change the stress response to it.
summary: Reframing does not erase difficulty, but it can reduce threat intensity and improve your sense of agency.
mechanism: Appraisal shapes whether the nervous system treats a situation as danger, challenge, loss, or opportunity.
limitations: Reappraisal is not always enough on its own, especially in chronic or structural stressors.
skeptical_view: Poorly done reappraisal can become avoidance or self-deception.
practical_takeaway: When stressed, ask what else the situation could mean besides danger or personal failure.
reflection_prompt: Which recurring stressor could become lighter if you changed the story you tell yourself about it?
source_2_title: How to Think Like a Roman Emperor

title: One message per talk
claim: Audiences usually remember one clear thesis better than several medium-strength points.
summary: Coverage feels satisfying to the speaker, but recall depends on compression and clarity.
mechanism: Working memory is limited. A single organizing message gives the audience a stable structure to attach details to.
limitations: Some technical settings genuinely require more detail. Simplicity must not distort substance.
skeptical_view: This principle is often used lazily to justify oversimplification.
practical_takeaway: Write the one sentence you want remembered, then test every slide against it.
reflection_prompt: What presentation of yours is trying to say too many things at once?

title: Stories beat abstractions
claim: Concrete stories are usually easier to remember than abstract explanations.
summary: Stories provide sequence, causality, and imagery, which improves retention and engagement.
mechanism: Narratives package information into units the mind can simulate and recall more easily than disembodied claims.
limitations: Stories can oversimplify and distort if they are treated as proof rather than illustration.
skeptical_view: A vivid anecdote is memorable, but it is not automatically representative.
practical_takeaway: Start explanations with one concrete case, then generalize.
reflection_prompt: Where do you explain too abstractly when a specific example would make the idea land?

title: Sleep pressure and adenosine
claim: Caffeine reduces the feeling of sleep pressure, but it does not replace sleep.
summary: It can improve alertness temporarily while leaving the underlying sleep debt unresolved.
mechanism: Adenosine builds during wakefulness; caffeine blocks its signaling rather than restoring biological recovery.
limitations: Individual responses vary with dose, timing, genetics, and sleep history.
skeptical_view: People often generalize from short-term productivity gains and ignore longer-term disruption.
practical_takeaway: Use caffeine strategically and avoid treating it as a substitute for recovery.
reflection_prompt: Where are you using stimulation to compensate for a recovery problem that should be fixed directly?
related_topics: circadian rhythm, light exposure, sleep debt

title: Morning light anchors the clock
claim: Light soon after waking helps anchor circadian timing.
summary: This can improve wakefulness cues and make the sleep-wake cycle more stable.
mechanism: Morning light acts on the biological clock through retinal pathways that influence circadian alignment.
limitations: Latitude, weather, schedule, and individual chronotype matter. It is not a perfect fix for every energy issue.
skeptical_view: Do not turn this into a ritual obsession; consistency matters more than perfection.
practical_takeaway: Get outdoor light soon after waking when practical.
reflection_prompt: What part of your daily energy might improve through timing rather than more discipline?

title: Expected value over single outcomes
claim: A good decision can still have a bad outcome.
summary: Under uncertainty, you should judge choices by process and expected value, not by one realized result.
mechanism: Noise and probability separate decision quality from outcome quality. Overweighting outcomes encourages superstition and hindsight bias.
limitations: Expected value is not the only criterion. Risk tolerance, downside asymmetry, and ethics also matter.
skeptical_view: People sometimes invoke expected value to excuse sloppy execution.
practical_takeaway: After an outcome, ask whether the decision was sensible given what you knew at the time.
reflection_prompt: Where are you being unfair to yourself because you are confusing a bad result with a bad decision?
related_topics: base rates, hindsight bias, probabilistic thinking

title: Base rates first
claim: Before focusing on the special details of a case, start with what usually happens in similar cases.
summary: Reference-class thinking reduces the pull of seductive anecdotes and exceptional narratives.
mechanism: Base rates anchor judgment in broader patterns before you layer on case-specific information.
limitations: Base rates can mislead when the reference class is poorly chosen or genuinely irrelevant.
skeptical_view: Some people use base rates mechanically and underweight real unique information.
practical_takeaway: In forecasts, identify the closest comparison class before reasoning from the inside view.
reflection_prompt: Which predictions of yours rely too much on story and too little on typical outcomes?
related_topics: inside vs outside view, planning fallacy, forecasting

title: Frontier claims need stronger filters
claim: The more novel and sensational a claim is, the higher your evidence threshold should be.
summary: Interesting is not the same as actionable, and early findings often fail to generalize.
mechanism: Novelty attracts attention and publication energy, but replication, external validity, and practical significance often lag behind the headline.
limitations: A conservative filter can make you slow to adopt genuinely useful innovations.
skeptical_view: Skepticism is valuable, but reflexive dismissal can become its own bias.
practical_takeaway: For every exciting claim, ask: what was measured, in whom, how large was the effect, and has it been replicated?
reflection_prompt: Which category of claims do you accept too quickly because they sound smart, technical, or edgy?

title: Retrieval beats rereading
claim: Testing yourself usually builds memory better than passively rereading notes.
summary: Active retrieval strengthens recall pathways and exposes what you do not actually know yet.
mechanism: Memory improves when the brain has to reconstruct information rather than just re-expose itself to it.
limitations: Retrieval works best when the material was encoded reasonably well in the first place.
skeptical_view: People sometimes over-quiz themselves without enough feedback or explanation.
practical_takeaway: After reading, close the page and write the key ideas from memory before checking.
reflection_prompt: What are you consuming repeatedly without ever testing whether it stuck?
related_topics: spacing effect, desirable difficulty, active recall
